introduction.heading: How FullFill Works
introduction.content: FullFill provides prescription cost transparency using only publicly available data sources. We believe patients and clinicians deserve to know where our information comes from and how we calculate our estimates.
key_points: 100% public data sources — no proprietary claims databases
Evidence-based algorithms validated by clinical pharmacists
Regular updates from authoritative government and industry sources

categories.description: Real-world prescription costs from national databases

sources.name: GoodRx Pricing Database
sources.description: Aggregated cash-pay pricing from 70,000+ U.S. pharmacies. Provides cost ranges showing price variation across retail and independent pharmacies.
sources.how_we_use_it: Primary source for cost range estimates (low/high bounds)

sources.name: NADAC (National Average Drug Acquisition Cost)
sources.description: Official survey-based benchmark of pharmacy acquisition costs. Updated weekly by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services.
sources.how_we_use_it: Validation benchmark for pricing reasonableness, especially for generic medications

sources.name: Medicare Part D Spending Data
sources.description: Annual prescription drug spending and utilization data for Medicare beneficiaries.
sources.how_we_use_it: Trend analysis and validation of pricing ranges for Medicare population

categories.description: Coverage information from Medicare, Medicaid, and commercial plans

sources.name: CMS Medicare Part D Plan Finder
sources.description: Official Medicare tool showing formulary tier placement, prior authorization requirements, and step therapy protocols across all Medicare Part D plans.
sources.how_we_use_it: Reference standard for formulary tier assignments; model for typical commercial plan structures

sources.name: State Medicaid Preferred Drug Lists
sources.description: State-specific lists of preferred medications with coverage criteria. Links to all 50+ state Medicaid formularies.
sources.how_we_use_it: Identification of medications requiring prior authorization; understanding state-level coverage variations

sources.name: Express Scripts National Preferred Formulary
sources.description: Publicly available formulary from one of the largest pharmacy benefit managers, representing typical commercial plan design.
sources.how_we_use_it: Proxy for commercial insurance formulary patterns; identification of step therapy agents

categories.description: Official FDA and NIH databases for medication identification

sources.name: FDA National Drug Code (NDC) Directory
sources.description: Official FDA database of all marketed drugs with unique identifiers. Updated daily.
sources.how_we_use_it: Drug identification and classification; linking brand to generic equivalents

sources.name: FDA Orange Book
sources.description: Approved Drug Products with Therapeutic Equivalence Evaluations. Identifies which generic drugs are therapeutically equivalent to brand-name drugs.
sources.how_we_use_it: Identification of therapeutically equivalent alternatives; determination of brand-only vs. generic availability

sources.name: RxNorm (National Library of Medicine)
sources.description: Standardized nomenclature for medications providing normalized names and unique identifiers.
sources.how_we_use_it: Medication standardization; therapeutic class assignments; clinical equivalence grouping

categories.description: Peer-reviewed research on drug pricing and patient access

sources.name: Health Affairs Journal
sources.description: Leading health policy journal with peer-reviewed research on prescription drug pricing, insurance design, and cost transparency.
sources.how_we_use_it: Evidence base for fill risk scoring; understanding patient behavior related to cost barriers

sources.name: JAMA Network - Medication Costs Collection
sources.description: Clinical research on medication costs, insurance coverage gaps, and patient access barriers.
sources.how_we_use_it: Clinical validation of cost impact on health outcomes; correlation between cost and adherence

sources.name: IQVIA Institute Reports
sources.description: Annual reports on medicine spending, patient assistance programs, and healthcare trends using pharmacy transaction data.
sources.how_we_use_it: Industry benchmarks for prior authorization approval rates; understanding market trends

sections.title: Cost Estimation
details: We provide cost ranges (low/high), not single estimates, because prescription prices vary significantly by pharmacy, insurance, and location
Low estimate: Minimum price using discount programs at low-cost pharmacies (e.g., Walmart, Costco)
High estimate: Typical retail price at major chains without insurance or discounts
All costs quoted for standard 30-day supply at typical maintenance dose
Cross-validated against multiple sources (GoodRx + NADAC + Medicare data)

sections.title: Fill Risk Scoring
details: Fill risk predicts access barriers, not cost — it answers 'Will my patient encounter problems getting this medication?'
Specialty-aware algorithm: Different specialties (dermatology, primary care, urgent care) face different insurance barriers
Four key barriers: Prior authorization required, brand-only (no generic), high formulary tier, step therapy required
Validated against academic research showing prescription abandonment rates
Output: LOW (likely to fill), MEDIUM (some barriers), HIGH (significant barriers expected)

sections.title: Confidence Scores
details: Confidence scores (0-100) reflect estimate reliability, NOT coverage likelihood
Generic medications = higher confidence (more predictable pricing)
Brand/specialty drugs = lower confidence (high price variability, manufacturer assistance unknown)
Version 1 uses public data (confidence 50-65 for best estimates)
Version 2 will integrate real-time benefit checks for patient-specific estimates (confidence 80-100)

sections.title: Clinical Validation
details: All medications reviewed by board-certified clinical pharmacists
Therapeutic class assignments validated against clinical guidelines
Alternative medications reviewed for clinical appropriateness
Formulary tier assignments validated across 50+ Medicare Part D plans
Continuous quality monitoring and quarterly data updates

qa_pairs.question: Will my patient pay exactly what FullFill shows?
qa_pairs.answer: Not exactly. We show cost ranges, not precise patient-specific amounts. Actual costs depend on your patient's specific insurance plan, deductible status, pharmacy choice, and eligibility for manufacturer assistance. Version 2 (coming soon) will provide patient-specific estimates using real-time benefit checks.

qa_pairs.question: Why do some medications have wide cost ranges?
qa_pairs.answer: Prescription prices vary 80%+ between pharmacies for the same drug. Brand-name and specialty medications also have highly variable pricing depending on insurance coverage and manufacturer copay cards. Wide ranges reflect this real-world variability.

qa_pairs.question: How often is the data updated?
qa_pairs.answer: Pricing data: Quarterly. Medicare formularies: Annual (October). Medicaid formularies: Quarterly. FDA drug databases: Monthly. Generic availability: Monthly. We refresh all data on a rolling schedule to maintain accuracy.

qa_pairs.question: Does FullFill know my patient's specific insurance plan?
qa_pairs.answer: Not in Version 1. We use aggregated formulary data representing typical Medicare, Medicaid, and commercial plans. Version 2 will integrate with insurance systems to provide patient-specific coverage information when the patient consents to share their insurance details.

qa_pairs.question: Why do confidence scores differ between medications?
qa_pairs.answer: Generic drugs in lower formulary tiers have predictable, stable pricing = high confidence. Brand-only specialty medications have variable pricing, unknown manufacturer assistance eligibility, and complex insurance coverage = low confidence. The score reflects data quality and price predictability.

qa_pairs.question: What if the actual cost is different from the estimate?
qa_pairs.answer: This is expected and why we provide ranges with confidence scores. Encourage patients to: (1) Call their pharmacy for exact quotes, (2) Compare prices using GoodRx/RxSaver, (3) Ask about manufacturer copay cards for brand drugs, (4) Check patient assistance program eligibility.

limitations.heading: What We Can't Predict
limitations.disclaimer: FullFill provides cost transparency based on public data, but cannot predict:
cannot_predict: Patient-specific copay amounts (depends on individual plan design)
Whether your patient's deductible has been met
Eligibility for manufacturer copay assistance or patient assistance programs
Exact prior authorization approval likelihood for individual patients
Plan-specific formulary variations (10,000+ unique plans in the U.S.)
Pharmacy network restrictions or preferred pharmacy discounts
Real-time drug shortages or temporary availability issues

best_practices.heading: Best Practices for Prescribers
recommendations: Use FullFill cost estimates to inform initial prescribing decisions
Counsel patients that actual costs may vary and to check with their pharmacy
For brand medications, inform commercially insured patients about manufacturer copay cards
If patient reports high costs, consider therapeutic alternatives shown in FullFill
For medications requiring prior authorization, warn patients about potential delays
Encourage uninsured/underinsured patients to use discount programs (GoodRx, RxSaver)

transparency_commitment.heading: Our Commitment to Transparency
transparency_commitment.content: FullFill was built on the principle that prescription pricing should not be a mystery. Every cost estimate, formulary tier, and risk score in our application is backed by documented public data sources. We publish our methodology openly so clinicians and patients can understand — and trust — the information we provide.
principles: Public data only: No proprietary claims databases or black-box algorithms
Documented sources: Every data point traced to authoritative government or academic source
Open methodology: All scoring algorithms published and explained
Clinical validation: Pharmacist review of all medication data
User feedback: Continuous improvement based on real-world prescriber and patient experiences
No conflicts of interest: Not sponsored by pharmaceutical companies, PBMs, or insurers

call_to_action.heading: Questions About Our Data?
call_to_action.content: We welcome feedback from clinicians and patients. If you encounter pricing discrepancies or have questions about our methodology, please reach out.

contact_methods.description: Use the feedback button in the FullFill application to report data issues
View our open-source code and methodology documentation

next_version_features: Real-time pharmacy benefit checks (RTPB) for patient-specific estimates
